step_by_step_thinking:
I agree with the initial assessment provided by @agent2. The method to calculate the infant mortality rate involves adding neonatal deaths (those occurring under 28 days post-birth) and those who die during their first year but after the age of 28 days. Summarizing these gives us 56 + 34 = 90 fatalities among infants from birth through the end of their first year. 

Given there were approximately 456 births in April alone based on the information, we need to infer annual figures; however, since only partial data beyond this month isn't available here explicitly stating otherwise, sticking strictly to calculations based on what’s presented ensures accuracy without unfounded extrapolations.

Therefore,
My final answer is A. 197.4 per thousand live births as indicated earlier. Option B, C & D do not align correctly according to our defined parameters using precise statistics outlined initially. Each
raw_response:
I agree with the initial assessment provided by @agent2. The method to calculate the infant mortality rate involves adding neonatal deaths (those occurring under 28 days post-birth) and those who die during their first year but after the age of 28 days. Summarizing these gives us 56 + 34 = 90 fatalities among infants from birth through the end of their first year. 

Given there were approximately 456 births in April alone based on the information, we need to infer annual figures; however, since only partial data beyond this month isn't available here explicitly stating otherwise, sticking strictly to calculations based on what’s presented ensures accuracy without unfounded extrapolations.

Therefore,
My final answer is A. 197.4 per thousand live births as indicated earlier. Option B, C & D do not align correctly according to our defined parameters using precise statistics outlined initially.
